html:
CXI.TO: Cleaned-Up U.S. FX Infrastructure at a Kiosk Multiple

At the latest available TSX quote of C$24.39 on May 15, 2026, Currency Exchange International is valued around C$147 million. That price still treats CXI like a sleepy retail foreign-currency kiosk business with a messy Canadian bank attached. The Canadian bank issue is now largely gone. Exchange Bank of Canada was discontinued, continued out of the Bank Act, and dissolved in late April 2026, with CXI announcing the completed exit on May 4, 2026. The market has not fully adjusted to the cleaner company left behind: a U.S.-focused foreign exchange, banknote, and payments processor with scarce compliance infrastructure, proprietary software, embedded bank and credit union relationships, excess capital, and an active buyback.

The old thesis was partly wrong: there was no big EBC sale to surface a hidden Schedule I bank license. The better thesis is cleaner and more repeatable. EBC was a drag, not the prize. In fiscal 2025, continuing operations generated $72.4 million of revenue, $23.3 million of EBITDA, and $14.0 million of net income, while discontinued EBC operations lost $3.7 million after tax. On continuing diluted EPS of US$2.29, the stock is at roughly 8x earnings before giving much credit to excess capital or payments growth. That is too cheap for a company whose core payments revenue is now inflecting.

The key change is mix. In Q1 2026, payments revenue grew 49% year over year, payment trading volume grew 46%, and CXI processed 59,804 payment transactions representing $2.16 billion of business volume. Payments were 27% of revenue in Q1, up from 18% a year earlier. This is not a consumer app story. It is a boring workflow story: small banks and credit unions do not want to build FX compliance, correspondent banking, exotic currency, wire, check clearing, and teller-facing software themselves. CXI plugs into core banking systems, APIs, and its CXIFX platform, then becomes the outsourced FX desk.

That is the variant perception. The market sees banknotes and travel cyclicality. The upside is that CXI has already paid for much of the integration and compliance infrastructure needed to scale a higher-quality payments business. Management said on the Q4 call that payment integrations are already a majority of the channel, that the pipeline is strong, and that the company is adding new clients without materially expanding the sales team. It also has an early Fed Direct software-fee pilot with four U.S. financial institutions. That is not yet material, but it points to a potential SaaS-like fee stream layered on top of transaction spread income.

The banknote business still matters, and the market may be over-penalizing it. Fiscal 2025 banknotes revenue grew 3% to $60.1 million, even before a cleaner post-EBC structure. Q1 2026 banknotes fell 11% because of weaker Canadian inbound travel, higher sourcing costs for certain currencies, and non-recurring prior-year trades. Those are real headwinds, but they are not proof of secular collapse. CXI added 21 new financial institution banknote clients in Q1, operates OnlineFX in 47 states plus D.C., and still benefits from banks retreating from low-volume, high-compliance FX service lines. Retail cash exchange is not a sexy business, but scarcity and service reliability matter when competitors do not want the regulatory burden.

The balance sheet gives the thesis unusual protection for a microcap. At January 31, 2026, CXI had $84 million of equity, $74 million of working capital, no revolver borrowings, a $40 million unused line, and $34 million held in AAA-rated money market funds. Not all cash should be treated as surplus because banknotes are inventory, but the balance sheet is far stronger than the market multiple implies. The company repurchased 151,000 shares for $2.5 million in Q1 2026 after buying back 312,300 shares in fiscal 2025, and the current NCIB allows repurchases of up to 359,617 shares, or 10% of the public float. With only about 6 million shares outstanding and insiders owning roughly 29%, consistent buybacks can move per-share value.

The retail and social context is almost the point: there is very little. Average trading volume is tiny, short interest is negligible, and discussion is mostly travelers asking whether CXI is a legitimate place to order yen or euros, not investors underwriting a payments platform. The May 2025 upgrade to OTCQX under CURN helps U.S. access, and management has publicly discussed a possible Nasdaq move around 2027 after the Canada exit. A Nasdaq uplisting is not needed for the stock to work, but it is a plausible re-rating catalyst for a Florida-based U.S. operating company stranded on the TSX.

The main risks are straightforward. Banknotes are seasonal and travel-sensitive. Canadian inbound weakness is a real near-term drag. Payments growth may carry lower incremental margin than bulls expect because bank service charges rise with volume. Compliance failure would be severe. CEO Randolph Pinna’s 23% ownership is aligned but also creates control risk, especially alongside a shareholder rights plan. Liquidity is poor, and the stock can stay cheap because larger funds cannot build positions easily. Stablecoins and real-time payment rails are long-term threats, but CXI’s niche is less about generic money movement and more about regulated workflow, bank integration, exotic currency, settlement, and outsourced compliance.

The setup is attractive because the market is valuing a cleaned-up, capital-rich, U.S. FX infrastructure business at a post-mess multiple while the forward mix is improving. If payments simply hold the recent trajectory and banknotes stabilize, CXI should earn through the EBC exit noise and show that fiscal 2025 continuing earnings were not a one-off. At that point, 8x continuing earnings is too low.

At C$24.39 today:

Bear case: C$18, down 26%. Banknotes remain weak, payments growth slows sharply, post-EBC cost absorption limits margins, and the market values CXI near 1x book or 7x depressed earnings.

Base case: C$36, up 48%. CXI earns roughly US$2.30 to US$2.50 per share on a cleaner continuing basis, payments keep growing, buybacks reduce the float, and the stock gets only a modest 10x to 11x earnings multiple.

Bull case: C$55, up 126%. Payments compound near 30%+, banknotes recover with travel and new financial institution wins, Fed Direct/software fees become visible, Nasdaq optionality improves the shareholder base, and EPS power moves toward US$3.25 to US$3.50 with a 11x to 12x multiple.
